perturbation.original_text:
(a) This Agreement will be governed in all respects by the laws of the State of Ohio (without regard to conflicts of law provisions), as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within the State of Ohio between Ohio residents.
(b) Any dispute between the Parties concerning the scope or interpretation of this Agreement shall be submitted to binding arbitration in accordance with the Rules of Commercial Arbitration of the American Arbitration Association in effect on the date that a dispute is submitted to arbitration (the "Rules"). The arbitration shall be held in Canton, Ohio, and shall be before a panel of three arbitrators, one chosen by each of the Parties and a third chosen by the two arbitrators so chosen by the Parties. Not less than fifteen (15) days prior to the arbitration, each Party shall submit to the other the documents and a list of witnesses it intends to interview or call in the arbitration. The arbitrators shall apply the substantive law of the State of Ohio with regard to any dispute that becomes the subject of arbitration, and the arbitrators will be so instructed. The arbitrators shall issue a written opinion stating the findings of fact and the conclusions of law upon which the decision is based. The decision of the arbitrators shall be final and binding.
perturbation.changed_text:
(a) This Agreement will be governed in all respects by the laws of the State of Ohio (without regard to conflicts of law provisions), as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within the State of Ohio between Ohio residents.
(b) Any dispute between the Parties concerning the scope or interpretation of this Agreement shall be submitted to binding arbitration in accordance with the Rules of Commercial Arbitration of the American Arbitration Association in effect on the date that a dispute is submitted to arbitration (the "Rules"). The arbitration shall be held in Canton, Ohio, and shall be before a panel of three arbitrators, one chosen by each of the Parties and a third chosen by the two arbitrators so chosen by the Parties. Not less than fifteen (15) days prior to the arbitration, each Party shall submit to the other the documents and a list of witnesses it intends to interview or call in the arbitration. The arbitrators shall apply the substantive law of the State of Delaware with regard to any dispute that becomes the subject of arbitration, and the arbitrators will be so instructed. The arbitrators shall issue a written opinion stating the findings of fact and the conclusions of law upon which the decision is based. The decision of the arbitrators shall be final and binding.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the substantive law applied by the arbitrators from Ohio to Delaware, the agreement creates a contradiction. The governing law clause specifies Ohio law, but the arbitration clause specifies Delaware law, which causes a confusion and conflict in the agreement that introduces a structural flaw.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 2711.05 outlines procedures related to arbitration agreements. Changing the applicable law within the arbitration clause undermines the intent and enforceability of the agreement under Ohio law, creating uncertainty about which state's laws govern the arbitration process. The law expects that the jurisdiction's law will be consistent throughout the document.

Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2711.05 | Hearing of application. Ohio Revised Code / Title 27 Courts-General Provisions-Special Remedies / Chapter 2711 Arbitration Effective: October 1, 1953 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Any application to the court of common pleas under sections 2711.01 to 2711.15 , inclusive, of the Revised Code, shall be made and heard in the manner provided by law for the making and hearing of motions, except as otherwise expressly provided in such sections. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1953 – House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1953 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that Ohio law governs any disputes. The changed contract, however, specifies that Delaware law will be applied by the arbitrators, which directly contradicts the original agreement and introduces a structural flaw, as defined in the prompt, by creating a conflict between the governing law and the law applied in arbitration, causing uncertainty and undermining enforceability under Ohio law.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 Confidentiality (a) "Confidential Information" means all forms of confidential information, including technical information and business information, disclosed by one Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") during the Term in connection with this Agreement, that is identified as confidential or is information that is of a nature that is customarily regarded as confidential, whether disclosed in electronic, tangible, oral or visual form; provided that oral or visual disclosures shall be deemed confidential only if they are confirmed as confidential in writing by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter, or if the Receiving Party should reasonably know that such visual or oral disclosures are intended to be confidential. Confidential Information shall not include such information that: (i) as of the date of disclosure is known to the Receiving Party or its Affiliates, as shown by written documentation; (ii) was independently developed by the Receiving Party or its Affiliates without access to the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (iii) as of the date of disclosure is in, or subsequently enters, the public domain, through no fault of the Receiving Party; or (iv) as of the date of disclosure or thereafter is obtained from a third party free from any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 Confidentiality (a) "Confidential Information" means all forms of confidential information, including technical information and business information, disclosed by one Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") during the Term in connection with this Agreement, that is identified as confidential or is information that is of a nature that is customarily regarded as confidential, whether disclosed in electronic, tangible, oral or visual form; provided that oral or visual disclosures shall be deemed confidential only if they are confirmed as confidential in writing by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter. Confidential Information shall not include such information that: (i) as of the date of disclosure is known to the Receiving Party or its Affiliates, as shown by written documentation; (ii) was independently developed by the Receiving Party or its Affiliates without access to the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (iii) as of the date of disclosure is in, or subsequently enters, the public domain, through no fault of the Receiving Party; or (iv) as of the date of disclosure or thereafter is obtained from a third party free from any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original confidentiality clause states that the receiver should have reasonably known that visual or oral disclosures are intended to be confidential. Removing this stipulation allows for confusion, giving the receiving party an out if they were legitimately unaware that they were receiving confidential information. This adds a structural flaw because it undermines the agreement's protections of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 1333.61 protects trade secrets by preventing their misappropriation. By removing the "should reasonably know" clause, the contract weakens the protection of confidential information. If the receiving party 'should've reasonably known' about the trade secret, it's still covered by the law. By removing it, the contract introduces a structural flaw that undercuts this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the 'should reasonably know' clause, weakening the protection of confidential information. According to ORC § 1333.61, misappropriation includes the acquisition of a trade secret by someone who 'knows or has reason to know' it was acquired improperly. By deleting the 'should reasonably know' language, the contract provides an avenue for the receiving party to claim ignorance, even when they reasonably should have known the information was confidential, undermining the protection offered by the law, as it removes a route in which the contract would consider an entity to have misappropriated confidential information. This creates a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction.
